sourceMovie.title: Babe
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Babe works as a fantasy movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The fantasy and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: All Dogs Go to Heaven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Babe clicked for you, All Dogs Go to Heaven (1989) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: How the Grinch Stole Christmas
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Babe clicked for you, How the Grinch Stole Christmas (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Babe?
faq.answer: Try All Dogs Go to Heaven, How the Grinch Stole Christmas, Elf—each shares DNA with Babe while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Babe?
faq.answer: Babe (1995) is best described as Fantasy, Drama, Comedy, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Babe part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Babe Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
